toc.title: $ ls ./chapters
toc.hint: Click a chapter to jump directly
intro.title: Learn C# Programming
intro.subtitle: From zero to building real applications
intro.problem_title: You want to build something. But where do you start?
intro.problem_answer: C# is one language that does all of this — and more.
intro.csharp_fact2: Runs on .NET — a free, open-source platform
intro.csharp_fact3: Works on Windows, macOS, and Linux
intro.what_can_build: What can you build with C#?
intro.why1_desc: Regular updates, latest features like records, pattern matching, and nullable types
intro.why2_desc: The compiler catches most bugs before you even run the code
intro.why3_desc: Millions of developers, huge ecosystem of libraries and packages
intro.why4_desc: Top 5 most in-demand languages in enterprise and game development
intro.hello_world_desc: C# is expressive — in modern .NET you don't even need a class to get started.
intro.hello_world_note: Just one line to print to the console. That's it.
intro.takeaway_title: C# is versatile, beginner-friendly, and industry-standard
intro.takeaway_desc: Whatever you want to build, C# has the tools, the community, and the job market to back you up.
setup.title: Setting Up Your Environment
setup.subtitle: Three tools. Five minutes. Ready to code.
setup.tool1_desc: The compiler and runtime that builds and runs C# programs
setup.tool2_desc: A lightweight, fast code editor that works on every OS
setup.tool3_desc: Adds IntelliSense, debugging, project management, and test runner to VS Code
setup.install_sdk_desc: Use your system's package manager to install the .NET 8 SDK:
setup.install_sdk_note: Or download the installer directly from
setup.verify_desc: Open a new terminal window and run:
setup.vscode_step2_desc: Search and install these extensions from the Extensions panel:
setup.vscode_step3_desc: C# Dev Kit requires a free Microsoft account for full IntelliSense features
setup.create_project_desc: Use the .NET CLI to scaffold a new console application:
setup.structure_note: .NET 6+ uses top-level statements — no boilerplate class or Main() needed.
setup.takeaway_title: Environment set up. First program running.
setup.takeaway_point2: VS Code with C# Dev Kit configured
setup.takeaway_point3: First console project created and running
setup.takeaway_desc: Next up: learning the building blocks of the C# language.
variables.title: Variables & Data Types
variables.subtitle: How programs store and manage information
variables.problem_question: How does a program remember information?
variables.problem_bullet1: A calculator needs to hold numbers between operations
variables.problem_bullet2: A login form needs to remember the username you typed
variables.problem_bullet3: A game needs to track score, health, and player name
variables.concept_desc: A variable is a named storage location in memory. The type defines what kind of data it can hold.
variables.conversion_desc: Sometimes you need to convert a value from one type to another.
variables.conv_implicit_desc: automatic, safe — no data loss (int → double)
variables.conv_explicit_desc: manual, may lose data — use (type) syntax
variables.conv_convert_desc: safe conversion from string or other types
variables.conv_parse_desc: parse a string directly into a numeric type
variables.const_desc: Declared with const — the value is fixed at compile time and can never change.
variables.const_example_note: e.g. const double Pi = 3.14159; — use for values that never change
variables.interp_desc: Prefix a string with $ to embed expressions directly inside curly braces.
variables.takeaway_store: Variables store data in named memory locations
variables.takeaway_type: Types define what data is valid (int, string, bool…)
variables.takeaway_const: const locks a value — it can never be reassigned
variables.takeaway_interp: String interpolation embeds values cleanly with $""
variables.takeaway_cta: Next: making decisions with Control Flow →
control.title: Control Flow
control.subtitle: Directing program execution based on conditions
control.problem_question: How does a program make decisions?
control.problem_bullet1: A banking app should reject a withdrawal if balance is insufficient
control.problem_bullet2: A game should show 'Game Over' only when health reaches zero
control.problem_bullet3: An app should display different menus for admin vs regular users
control.if_desc: Branches execute different code depending on whether a condition is true or false.
control.if_if_desc: Runs the block if the condition is true
control.if_elseif_desc: Checked only when the previous condition was false
control.if_else_desc: Fallback — runs when no condition above matched
control.op_lte_gte: Less than or equal / Greater than or equal
control.op_and: AND — both sides must be true
control.op_or: OR — at least one side must be true
control.switch_desc: A cleaner alternative to long if / else if chains when comparing one variable to many values.
control.switch_case_desc: Runs when the variable matches this value
control.switch_break_desc: Exits the switch — required after each case
control.switch_default_desc: Fallback when no case matches (like else)
control.ternary_desc: A compact one-line alternative to a simple if / else.
control.ternary_bullet1: Best for simple, short conditions — avoid nesting ternaries
control.ternary_bullet2: Result must be assigned to a variable or used in an expression
control.takeaway_switch: switch is cleaner for matching one value against many
control.takeaway_ternary: Ternary ? : compresses simple if/else to one line
loops.title: Loops
loops.subtitle: Automate repetition with elegance
loops.problem.title: The Problem
loops.problem.question: How do you repeat actions without writing the same code 100 times?
loops.problem.answer: Loops let you execute a block of code repeatedly until a condition is met.
loops.for.title: The for Loop
loops.for.init_desc: Runs once before the loop starts
loops.for.example_note: The loop variable i is scoped to the loop — it doesn't exist outside the braces.
loops.while.title: while and do-while
loops.while.while_point2: May run zero times if condition is false
loops.while.dowhile_point1: Runs the body at least once
loops.while.tip: Use while when you don't know how many iterations you need. Use for when you do.
loops.foreach.title: The foreach Loop
loops.foreach.point1: Iterates over every element in a collection
loops.foreach.point2: No index variable needed — cleaner code
loops.foreach.point3: Works with arrays, List<T>, and any IEnumerable
loops.foreach.point4: Cannot modify the collection while iterating
loops.break.title: Controlling Loop Flow
loops.break.break_desc: Exits the loop immediately — no more iterations run.
loops.break.continue_desc: Skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one.
loops.takeaway.title: Choose the Right Loop
loops.takeaway.for_when: You know the exact number of iterations
loops.takeaway.while_when: You loop until a condition changes
loops.takeaway.dowhile_when: The body must run at least once
loops.takeaway.summary: Loops automate repetition. Pick the one that best expresses your intent.
arrays.title: Arrays & Collections
arrays.subtitle: Store and manage groups of related data
arrays.problem.title: The Problem
arrays.problem.question: How do you store and work with a list of items without naming each one separately?
arrays.problem.answer: Arrays and collections let you group related values under a single name.
arrays.array.title: Arrays: Fixed-Size Containers
arrays.array.point1: Stores multiple values of the same type
arrays.array.point2: Fixed size — set at creation, cannot grow or shrink
arrays.array.point3: Zero-based index: first element is at index 0
arrays.array.point4: Access any element instantly via its index
arrays.array.example_note: Trying to access an index outside the array length throws an IndexOutOfRangeException.
arrays.list.title: List<T>: Dynamic Collections
arrays.list.list_point3: Best for most everyday use cases
arrays.methods.title: Common List<T> Methods
arrays.methods.add: Adds an item to the end
arrays.methods.insert: Inserts item at a specific index
arrays.methods.note: List<T> lives in System.Collections.Generic — add a using directive at the top of your file.
arrays.combined.title: Loops + Arrays Together
arrays.combined.desc: The real power comes from combining loops with arrays to process data.
arrays.combined.note: Start with scores[0] as the assumed max, then check each remaining element — a classic pattern.
arrays.takeaway.title: Arrays vs List<T>
arrays.takeaway.array_point3: Use for buffers, coordinates, lookup tables
arrays.takeaway.summary: When in doubt, use List<T>. Switch to an array only when size is truly fixed.
methods.title: Methods
methods.subtitle: Writing reusable, organized code
methods.problem.title: The Problem
methods.problem.issue2: Code is repeated in multiple places
methods.concept.title: What is a Method?
methods.concept.description: A method is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You define it once, call it many times.
methods.concept.part_return_desc: what value the method gives back (or void for nothing)
methods.concept.part_name_desc: a clear verb describing what it does
methods.concept.part_body_desc: the code that runs when called
methods.example1.title: Define & Call a Method
methods.example1.description: Define once, call anywhere — no duplication.
methods.example1.note: // static means it belongs to the program, not an object (more on that in OOP)
methods.params.title: Parameters & Return Values
methods.params.input_desc: Data passed into the method for it to work with.
methods.params.output_desc: Data the method sends back to the caller.
methods.params.note: A method can have zero or more parameters, and returns exactly one value (or void).
methods.example2.title: Method with Parameters
methods.example2.description: Parameters let methods work with different data each time they are called.
methods.overload.title: Method Overloading
methods.overload.description: Multiple methods can share the same name as long as their parameter types or count differ. The compiler picks the right one.
methods.void.title: void vs Return Type
methods.void.void_desc: Does something but gives nothing back.
methods.void.return_desc: Computes and gives a value back.
methods.void.rule: Rule: if a method has a return type other than void, every code path must have a return statement.
methods.takeaway.title: Key Takeaways
methods.takeaway.point1: Methods make code reusable — write once, use many times
methods.takeaway.point2: Keep methods small and focused on one task
methods.takeaway.point3: Use descriptive names: GetTotal, PrintReport, IsValid
methods.takeaway.point4: Use overloading to handle different input types cleanly
methods.takeaway.quote: A good method does one thing and does it well.
oop.title: Object-Oriented Programming
oop.subtitle: Modeling the real world in code
oop.problem.title: The Problem
oop.problem.description: How do you represent a real-world thing — like a student — in code?
oop.problem.solution_hint: // OOP answer: bundle related data + behavior into a class
oop.class_object.title: Classes & Objects
oop.class_object.class_desc: A blueprint — defines structure and behavior. Written once.
oop.class_object.object_desc: An instance of a class — actual data in memory.
oop.class_object.note: You define a class once and create as many objects as you need from it.
oop.example1.title: Defining a Class
oop.example1.description: Group related data (properties) and behavior (methods) into one class.
oop.properties.title: Properties vs Fields
oop.properties.tip: Tip: use auto-properties { get; set; } by default. Add logic in get/set only when needed.
oop.constructors.title: Constructors
oop.constructors.description: A constructor is a special method that runs automatically when an object is created.
oop.example2.title: Using Constructors
oop.example2.description: Constructors let you initialize an object's data in one clean step.
oop.access.title: Access Modifiers
oop.access.description: Control who can see and use each member of a class.
oop.access.public_desc: Anyone can access it — inside or outside the class
oop.access.private_desc: Only accessible inside the same class
oop.access.protected_desc: Accessible inside the class and in derived (child) classes
oop.inheritance.title: Inheritance
oop.inheritance.description: A class can inherit properties and methods from a parent class using the : syntax.
end.title: Congratulations!
end.recap_label: You've completed 8 chapters of C# basics:
end.footer: Keep building. Keep learning. The best code is the code you write tomorrow.
